Title: 'Rest now, Fisya': Best friend mourns college student stabbed 61 times
Article:
KOTA BARU: An emotional farewell from a close friend has captured the heartbreak surrounding the death of a 19-year-old college student, who was found with 61 stab wounds in Ketereh last Friday.
"Rest now, Fisya. I will keep every memory of us tucked safely in my heart," wrote a social media user, believed to be the best friend of Nurfisya Zulkifly.
The user, known as MissCandy on TikTok, shared her grief and longing following the sudden loss of her friend.
"I am her best friend. We were very close. It hurts so much to lose the person you care for most, the one who was always there through thick and thin.
"We used to share every problem. Now I have to learn to face this alone. Thank you for being the calmest home for me when I needed to seek refuge.
"If this longing could form a bridge, I would have crossed it to see you a long time ago. However, I know you are happy now in a place where there are no tears," she said.
She described Nurfisya as more than just a friend, noting that their bond was akin to family.
"I pray you find peace there. When my turn comes, do not forget to wait for me at the gates of heaven.
"My friend, you were the family I chose myself. Your departure has taught me that not everything we love stays by our side," she said.
In the incident at 12.30am last Friday, the body of the teenager was discovered in
Kampung Padang Simah, Ketereh
, bearing 61 stab wounds.
Kelantan police chief Datuk Mohd Yusoff Mamat had said that the victim is believed to have been murdered elsewhere before her body was left at the location.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Oil eases on signs US is loosening Iranian closure of Strait of Hormuz
Article:
BENGALURU: Oil prices eased on Tuesday after climbing by as much as six per cent in the previous session on signs the US Navy is loosening Iran's closure of the key Strait of Hormuz waterway, potentially opening up supply from the key Middle East producing area.
The US on Monday launched a new operation aimed at reopening Hormuz to shipping and Maersk said later its Alliance Fairfax, a US-flagged vehicle carrier, exited the Gulf via the strait accompanied by US military assets, easing some immediate supply disruption fears.
Brent oil futures for July fell 68 cents, or 0.6 per cent, to US$113.76 per barrel at 0100 GMT, after settling up 5.8 per cent on Monday. US West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude fell US$1.59, or 1.5 per cent, to US$104.83, after gaining 4.4 per cent in the previous session.
"The successful escorted exit of the Maersk-operated vessel has helped ease some immediate supply disruption fears," said Tim Waterer, chief market analyst at KCM Trade.
"It shows that limited safe passage is possible under current conditions and helps chip away at some of the worst-case supply disruption fears. However, it's still very much a one-off event rather than a full reopening," he said in an email.
Still, Iran launched attacks in the Gulf on Monday to counter the US move as they wrestle for control over the Strait of Hormuz, which connects the Gulf to wider markets and typically carries oil and gas supply equal to about 20 per cent of global demand every day.
Several commercial vessels were reportedly struck in the area, while a key oil port in the United Arab Emirates was set ablaze after an Iranian strike.
Trump's attempt to use the US Navy to free up shipping is the war's biggest escalation since a ceasefire was declared four weeks ago.
The US is pushing to open Hormuz to ease a massive disruption to global energy supplies since Iran mostly shut the strait after the US and Israel started the war on February 28.
On Monday, Chevron chairman and chief executive officer Mike Wirth said physical shortages in oil supply would begin appearing around the world because of the Hormuz closure.
Because of the disruptions, global oil stocks are approaching their lowest level in eight years, Goldman Sachs said on Monday, warning that the speed of depletion was becoming a concern as supplies remained restricted.
"With the world rapidly burning through commercial stockpiles, strategic reserves, and crude held in floating storage, the underlying supply squeeze remains a potent tailwind for oil prices," IG market analyst Tony Sycamore said in a note.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Lorry workshop in Sungai Besar catches fire, 80pct destroyed
Article:
SHAH ALAM: A lorry workshop conducting welding work at Parit 13, Sungai Panjang, Sungai Besar, caught fire yesterday.
Selangor Fire and Rescue Department Zone 7 chief Samsol Maarif Saibani said they received a call at 5.23pm.
A team of 13 personnel, along with fire engines from the Sungai Besar and Sabak Bernam Fire and Rescue Stations, was dispatched to the scene.
"Upon arrival, personnel found the fire involved a workshop and a lorry. The premises were about 80 per cent destroyed while the lorry was 30 per cent destroyed," he said when contacted last night.
He said firefighters then carried out extinguishing operations until the fire was completely put out.
No casualties were reported, and the cause of the fire is still under investigation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Australia's Regis to merge with Vault, creating US$7.7bil gold producer
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Australian gold producers Regis Resources and Vault Minerals will merge in an all-stock deal, the companies said on Tuesday, creating a combined entity valued at around A$10.7 billion (US$7.67 billion).
Regis will offer 0.6947 of its shares for each Vault share, implying a deal value of A$5.15 billion, according to a joint statement.
Regis's offer is at a 10.7 per cent premium to Vault's last close of A$4.50 on Monday. The deal is set to realise over A$500 million of corporate tax benefits and lower the cost of capital for the combined company, according to both firms.
Regis shareholders will own around 51 per cent of the combined entity and Vault shareholders will own the rest, the statement said.
The boards of both companies unanimously recommended the proposal, subject to no superior proposal emerging.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RM157m push to ease rising home pressure
Article:
ISKANDAR PUTERI:  The Johor government has delivered a firm signal on housing reform, stating that it will not leave the rakyat to struggle alone amid rising living costs and challenging property prices.
State Housing and Local Government Committee chairman Datuk Mohd Jafni Md Shukor said the enhanced Perumahan Kasih Johor (PKJ) 2026 initiative reflected a shift from policy on paper to real intervention on the ground, designed to directly ease the housing pressure faced by thousands of families.
"The Johor  government will not compromise on housing. When the cost of living rises and market prices become more difficult, we do not stand by and watch. We act, we improve and we ensure the people are protected," he said during the state legislative assembly sitting at the Sultan Ismail Building in Kota Iskandar here  yesterday.
The PKJ initiative is a Johor government housing support scheme that offers targeted financial aid to help eligible residents buy, move into, rent or renovate homes, with the aim of improving affordability and access to housing across the state.
[[nid:1432465]]
Under the revamped PKJ 2026 framework, several key adjustments have been introduced, including a higher eligibility ceiling for rental assistance, which is now extended to households earning up to RM10,000.
The move, Jafni said, recognises that housing stress is no longer confined to lower-income groups but is increasingly affecting the M40 segment in urban areas.
Rental assistance has also been recalibrated to reflect current market realities, with eligible recipients now able to receive support of up to RM2,000 per month.
Instead of incremental aid, beneficiaries will receive a one-off RM2,500 payment to provide immediate financial breathing space.
"For us, it is not just about helping people survive month to month. It is about giving them room to breathe," he said.
For first-time homebuyers, the scheme has also been expanded, with the eligible property price ceiling raised to RM650,000 to reflect current housing market conditions.
The adjustment is aimed at widening access to homeownership, particularly for young families and middle-income earners seeking to upgrade their living conditions.
The state has also enhanced assistance for those entering their first homes, ensuring more buyers are supported under similar price thresholds in line with market realities.
Jafni said the changes represent a broader policy direction that seeks to enable upward mobility, allowing residents to move from lower-cost housing to more comfortable and dignified living conditions.
"These are not small adjustments. They involve tens of thousands of beneficiaries and allocations running into hundreds of millions of ringgit," he said.
He added that RM40 million has been allocated for rental assistance, RM100 million for home purchases and RM17.83 million for home entry support.
He stressed that housing is not a peripheral issue but a matter of dignity and long-term security.
The state government, he said, will continue to refine policies, cut bureaucratic delays, and ensure that assistance reaches those who genuinely need it.
In Phase 1 of PKJ, some 1,020 recipients benefited from an allocation of RM4.38 million, marking an initial step by the state government to support Johor folk in owning homes and easing the burden of upfront housing costs.
The momentum continued in Phase 2, where the number of beneficiaries rose to 2,506, with allocations increasing to RM10.529 million. This phase extended support to first-time homebuyers and tenants facing rising rental pressures.
Overall, 3,802 Johoreans have benefited under PKJ, with total allocations amounting to RM14.909 million.
Phase 3 of the initiative is scheduled for disbursement in July.
Jafni said the growing number of PKJ beneficiaries reflected Johor's commitment to ensuring its policies translate into tangible benefits for the rakyat.
Under the leadership of Menteri Besar Datuk Onn Hafiz Ghazi,  he added, Johor remained committed to ensuring that economic growth was translated directly into the people's well-being.
"In housing, we will not slow down, we will not step back. The people must be protected, homes must be attainable and the future must be secured," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Small UK firms being pushed out of EU markets due to red tape and rising costs
Article:
LONDON: A UK body representing small businesses warned on Tuesday that small firms were being pushed out of European Union markets due to red tape, rising costs, and complex rules, highlighting the continuing effects of Brexit more than six years after the UK left the union.
The research from Britain's Federation of Small Businesses underlined the challenges of trading with the bloc, despite the current Labour government's push to reset economic and defence ties at a time of global uncertainty over tariffs and war.
"The EU should be a natural market for our small firms as it's so close and accessible ... it's a frustrating shame that many are currently questioning whether it's even worth the effort at all," FSB Policy Chair Tina McKenzie said in the statement.
Here are the FSB's findings:
* Three in 10 traders in small- and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) expect to reduce or stop EU trade altogether if current rules do not change
* Six in 10 small firms trading with the EU have faced significant barriers
* Of those importing and exporting goods, 85 per cent report problems
* The most commonly reported problems include customs documentation, physical inspections of goods, and product marking and labelling rules
* Some 75 per cent of small firms that have engaged with EU value-added tax systems report significant difficulties
* The FSB makes a series of recommendations to improve trading conditions
* These include mutual recognition of product standards between the two sides, a single digital customs system, a new UK-EU de minimis (low-value trade) deal
* They also include export grants of up to 2,000 pounds to help small firms cover the upfront costs and scrapping EU requirements for VAT middlemen
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: On Rain Rave debate, distinguish advisory concern from regulatory breach
Article:
THE debate surrounding the Rain Rave Water Music Festival 2026 is not merely about entertainment, but about how a constitutional, multi-religious nation calibrates moral concerns against governance principles and economic realities.
The concerns raised by Datuk Dr Muhamad Akmal Saleh reflect a segment of societal anxiety, yet the more pressing policy question is whether such objections are grounded in enforceable standards or framed through selective interpretation that risks distorting institutional balance.
Malaysia's constitutional framework is clear. Islam is the religion of the Federation, yet the nation is not governed as a theocracy. Public policy must therefore operate within a structure that accommodates diversity while respecting religious sensitivities.
This balance is not theoretical; it is embedded in regulatory practice, ministerial accountability, and legal oversight. Any criticism of a public event must therefore be assessed not by rhetorical description, but by verifiable compliance with laws, licensing conditions, and public conduct guidelines.
JAWI's position, as publicly articulated, highlights legitimate concerns regarding moral boundaries and social impact.
Islamic jurisprudence does not reject entertainment outright, but emphasises responsibility, moderation, and adherence to ethical limits.
The Bayan Linnas guidance (a series of publications by Federal Territories Mufti Office providing clear guidance on current issues from an Islamic perspective) reinforces this principle.
However, it is essential to distinguish between advisory concern and regulatory breach.
If an event complies with existing legal requirements, does not involve prohibited elements such as alcohol consumption, and adheres to safety and licensing standards, then the issue transitions from legality to perception.
Public policy cannot be governed solely by perception, particularly when perceptions vary across a plural society.
This distinction is critical. If regulatory decisions were driven exclusively by subjective discomfort, governance would become inconsistent and reactive.
It would set a precedent where any organised activity could be challenged based on narrative rather than evidence.
One might respectfully suggest that policy should be guided by structured criteria rather than volume of objection. Otherwise, institutional credibility risks being replaced by episodic sentiment management.
The economic dimension further complicates the debate. Tourism remains a significant contributor to Malaysia's GDP, employment generation, and small business sustainability.
Data from the Department of Statistics Malaysia and international tourism frameworks consistently demonstrate that large-scale events stimulate economic activity across multiple sectors.
The framing of economic benefit as secondary to moral concern creates a false dichotomy.
In practice, national policy must reconcile both. Economic vitality and ethical governance are not mutually exclusive; they are interdependent pillars of sustainable development.
Ministerial decisions supporting tourism-related events should therefore be evaluated through measurable outcomes: compliance with regulations, inclusivity, economic impact, and maintenance of public order.
To characterise such initiatives as inherently immoral without substantiated evidence risks oversimplification. It also raises questions about consistency.
If similar standards were applied uniformly, a wide spectrum of urban activities — from concerts to cultural festivals — would face equivalent scrutiny.
Selective application, however, suggests that the issue may be less about principle and more about positioning.
The rhetorical tone of certain criticisms also warrants reflection. Public expressions that invoke extreme outcomes, including hopes for disruptive natural events, may resonate politically but do not contribute to constructive policy discourse.
Governance requires proportionate response, evidence-based reasoning, and institutional respect. Emotional amplification, while effective in mobilising attention, rarely translates into sustainable policy outcomes.
At the same time, it would be inaccurate to dismiss moral concerns entirely. In a society where religion plays a central role, public sensitivity must be acknowledged.
The responsibility therefore lies with organisers and authorities to ensure that entertainment remains within acceptable ethical boundaries.
This includes clear guidelines on conduct, appropriate enforcement mechanisms, and transparent communication with stakeholders. A regulated framework that integrates both economic and ethical considerations is not only possible, but necessary.
The broader implication of this debate extends beyond a single event. It reflects how Malaysia negotiates identity, governance, and development in a complex socio-political landscape.
When narratives lean toward absolutism, they risk undermining the very institutional balance that defines the nation. Conversely, when governance is anchored in law, evidence, and inclusivity, it reinforces both credibility and cohesion.
Ultimately, the question is not whether entertainment should exist, but whether governance can manage it responsibly. Malaysia's policy framework already provides the tools to do so.
The challenge lies in applying them consistently, without succumbing to selective narratives or rhetorical escalation. A mature policy environment requires discipline, not dramatisation.
In this context, the Rain Rave debate should serve as a reminder that governance is not a contest of moral superiority, but a responsibility to uphold balance.
When institutions function as intended, they protect both values and progress.
When they are drawn into selective contestation, credibility becomes the first casualty.
* The writer is a current affairs observer
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Female hiker dies after falling into Sungai Betis in Gua Musang
Article:
GUA MUSANG: A 56-year-old female hiker drowned after slipping and falling into Sungai Betis while hiking at Pos Balar here.
District police chief Superintendent Sik Choon Foo said the police were notified of the incident at 3.21pm on May 3.
Initial investigations showed the victim was part of an 11-member group that began hiking on May 1.
He said the expedition covered the Gunung Gerah, Gunung Bilah, and Gunung Biyed areas.
"At about 7.30pm on May 2, the victim was crossing Sungai Betis in the dark when she slipped and fell into the river," he said when contacted.
"The group immediately launched a search, but she was not found that night."
Following the incident, the police coordinated a Search and Rescue operation with the Fire and Rescue Department to recover the body.
The operation involved a four-hour journey in a four-wheel-drive vehicle, followed by a nearly 10-hour trek to the location.
Sik added that the victim was found at about 8am on May 3, about 300 metres from the incident location. She was confirmed dead.
"The victim was a single mother from Kuala Lumpur," he said.
"Police and relevant agencies have taken further action at the location, and the case has been classified as a sudden death.
"We advise the public to prioritise safety, plan carefully, and hire certified guides when hiking, especially in high-risk forest and river areas," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: From airlines to banks: Australian, New Zealand firms feel heat of Gulf crisis
Article:
SYDNEY: Companies in Australia and New Zealand are beginning to signal the financial strain from the US-Israeli war on Iran, as higher fuel prices stoke inflation, dent business and consumer confidence, and weigh on corporate earnings.
Here are some of the companies in Australia and New Zealand that have flagged an impact from the Middle East conflict:
Air New Zealand:
New Zealand's flag carrier suspended its full-year earnings outlook in early March, and said it had raised fares due to volatility in the jet fuel markets - one of the first carriers to announce price increases.
On April 7, the airline said it would slash flights through May and June, affecting around 4 per cent of flights and 1 per cent of total passengers.
Auckland International Airport:
New Zealand's Auckland International Airport said flights from Auckland to the Middle East were disrupted.
The Middle Eastern routes saw an 81 per cent drop in passenger numbers and a 73 per cent reduction in seat capacity in March, compared with a year ago, the airport operator added.
a2 Milk
New Zealand's a2 Milk cut its fiscal 2026 profit outlook as higher freight costs due to the conflict and temporary supply chain disruptions affect the availability of its China-label infant milk formula product in its biggest market.
Cleanaway Waste Management:
The waste management company slashed its full-year operating earnings forecast by about A$20 million (RM57 million), largely reflecting higher costs, lower activity, and timing differences in cost recovery.
Cochlear:
Australian hearing implants maker Cochlear trimmed its 2026 profit forecast after weaker trading in developed markets, citing slower surgical volumes, fewer hearing-aid referrals and softer consumer sentiment.
The company said the Middle East war has added risks of order cancellations, delivery delays and higher receivables exposure, worsening margin pressure and restructuring costs.
Fletcher Building:
New Zealand's Fletcher Building said it faces indirect exposure to the Middle East conflict via supply chains, freight routes, energy costs, and the broader economic impact on construction demand across Australasia.
The construction materials maker expects to pass through costs to customers, increasing prices across divisions. Plastics, which the company says face immediate exposure, will see price hikes by up to 36 per cent, while other divisions will see 1 per cent — 5 per cent hikes.
Flight Centre Travel:
Australian corporate travel manager Flight Centre Travel said hostilities in the Middle East are temporarily disrupting international travel patterns, estimating an impact of around A$10 million to its Leisure segment profit in April.
The firm also expects potential FX headwinds in the fourth quarter on overseas profit translation given Australian dollar's strength, stating that its cost margin is down to 9.2 per cent after the third quarter with ongoing focuses on support role freeze, others.
Fonterra:
New Zealand's dairy producer said that the conflict was impacting its supply chain, and could increase its inventory levels and costs in the second half of the year, while also contributing to volatility in global commodity prices.
National Australia Bank:
National Australia Bank, said it expects to incur credit impairment charges of A$706 million (RM2 trillion) in the first half of fiscal 2026.
NAB said second-quarter interest-rate volatility, a weaker New Zealand dollar and the provisioning increase would reduce the group's common equity tier 1 capital ratio by about 20 basis points as of March 31.
It also expects to apply a 1.5 per cent discount to the first-half dividend reinvestment plan to raise up to A$1.8 billion to help shore up its balance sheet.
Orora:
Packaging company Orora trimmed its annual earnings forecast for French unit Saverglass and cancelled its share buyback programme, citing the impact of the war.
T
he company has also ceased bottle production at its glass production facility at Ras al Khaimah in the United Arab Emirates due to the closure of shipping routes.
Qantas
Qantas Airways, Australia's flag carrier, raised its fuel cost outlook for the second half of the year by up to A$800 million, and said it has not started its planned share buyback of A$150 million, citing sharply higher and volatile jet fuel prices.
To offset rising costs, Qantas is lifting fares and shifting flights toward stronger routes such as Paris and Rome, where demand remains firm, while cutting domestic capacity by about 5 percentage points in the June quarter.
Qube Holdings:
Qube expects its EBITA earnings impact of around A$10 million-A$20 million for fiscal 2026 due to the Middle East conflict.
However, the logistics firm said that the recent events could support an acceleration in investment in new alternative energy projects, which could be advantageous for the firm.
Virgin Australia:
Virgin Australia said it expects an increase in fuel costs, one of its largest expenses, of around A$30 million to A$40 million ($21.39 million to $28.52 million) for the second half of fiscal 2026.
The airlines in mid-March said that it was adjusting fares as rising costs across the aviation sector are "exacerbated by the situation in the Middle East."
Westpac:
Westpac, Australia's no. 2 bank by assets, said energy market shocks from the conflict were emerging as profit pressures over the first half of the financial year ended March 31, prompting the lender to increase credit provisions.
Westpac's net interest margin in its treasury and markets division was weaker amid interest-rate volatility linked to the conflict, with a weaker outlook already prompting higher credit provisioning.
Westpac's provisioning for potential bad debt is now at its highest point since the Covid-19 pandemic.
Woolworths:
Woolworths, the top Australian grocer, said the Middle East conflict has created significant uncertainty for customers and suppliers, compounding already acute cost-of-living pressures.
The firm also warned that fiscal 2026 domestic food segment earnings growth would no longer reach the upper end of the range due to fuel price pressures and customer retention investments.
Woolworths will also freeze shelf prices for 300 household staples for three months from May 1, as conflict-driven cost pressures on Australian suppliers push prices up across supermarkets.
Worley:
Worley said the adverse impact of the Middle East conflict on its underlying EBITA for fiscal 2026 is estimated to be in the range of A$30 million to A$40 million.
The Australian engineering firm warned that it was unlikely to achieve growth in underlying EBITA in fiscal 2026, but continued to target higher growth in aggregated revenue than in fiscal 2025. — REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ringgit steady against US dollar ahead of MPC decision
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The ringgit opened nearly unchanged against the US dollar from yesterday's close as markets await the latest decision by Bank Negara Malaysia's Monetary Policy Committee (MPC), scheduled for May 7.
At 8am, the domestic unit traded at 3.9540/9590 against the greenback compared with 3.9540/9575 at yesterday's close.
Bank Muamalat Malaysia Bhd chief economist Dr Mohd Afzanizam Abdul Rashid said while the market adopted a wait-and-see approach ahead of the MPC meeting, the exchange of fire in the Persian Gulf erupted between United States (US) and Iranian forces, potentially compromising the ceasefire.
"Escalation in West Asia has consequently seen global oil benchmark WTI and  Brent crude prices jumped 4.39 per cent and 5.80 per cent to US$106.42 per barrel and US$114.44 per barrel, respectively.
"Today, the ringgit is expected to depreciate against the US dollar in light of the latest developments. Clearly, market sentiment will continue to revolve around the war in West Asia and the ensuing oil supply shock, which would exert upward pressure on inflation," he told Bernama.
Meanwhile, the ringgit traded higher against a basket of major currencies.
It rose against the Japanese yen to 2.5151/5184 from 2.5186/5210 at yesterday's close, appreciated versus the British pound to 5.3498/3565 from 5.3612/3660, and strengthened vis-a-vis the euro to 4.6230/6289 from 4.6329/6370 previously.
The local currency was firmer against regional peers.
It advanced against the Singapore dollar to 3.0963/1005 from 3.1019/1049 at Monday's close, gained versus the Thai baht to 12.0740/0963 from 12.1460/1631, edged up versus the Indonesian rupiah to 227.3/227.6 from 227.3/227.6, but was flat vis-a-vis the Philippine peso at 6.42/6.43.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: JKJ increased to RM45.4m
Article:
ISKANDAR PUTERI:  The Johor government has expanded its cost-of-living support through the Jualan Kasih Johor initiative, increasing the allocation to RM45.4 million to benefit more than 750,000 families statewide.
The state government is also opening the door for appeals from those who may have been left out of the Bantuan Kasih Johor (BKJ) programme.
Menteri Besar Datuk Onn Hafiz Ghazi said the initiatives reflected Johor's continued commitment to ensure that no eligible recipient is left behind in its welfare programmes.
"Initially, RM15.4 million was allocated this year to implement Jualan Kasih Johor at 336 locations, targeting over two million people," he said during the Johor State Legislative Assembly sitting at the Sultan Ismail Building in Kota Iskandar here yesterday.
"However, following strong response and feedback from the grassroots, the allocation has been increased by RM30 million to RM45.4 million in total," he added.
Onn Hafiz (BN-Machap) was responding to questions from assemblymen, including Datuk Mohd Fuad Tukirin (BN-Bukit Naning) and Tan Eng Meng (BN-Pekan Nanas), on cost-of-living pressures and rakyat welfare measures.
[[nid:1432448]]
He said the state government, through ICT@Johor, is also ensuring that the BKJ database is carefully managed to avoid duplication while improving accuracy in identifying beneficiaries.
"If there are cases of exclusion, the state is prepared to consider appeals.
"Through ICT at the state level, we are ensuring that such appeals can be channelled through the proper mechanisms," he said.
"At the same time, we also want to ensure there is no duplication in the recipient list," he added.
Onn Hafiz said the BKJ Phase 4 programme, with an allocation of RM138 million  covering 22 initiatives, is supported by comprehensive data coordination involving state executive councillors and relevant agencies.
He said data for specific groups such as orphans, persons with disabilities and senior citizens are obtained through the state executive councillor for Islamic Religious Affairs, the Social Welfare Department and district offices.
"For example, for orphans, we work with the relevant executive councillor and the welfare department. For people with disabilities and senior citizens, data is also channelled through the proper agencies and district offices that coordinate at the ground level."
Onn Hafiz added that data for  most  beneficiaries had  been consolidated through these coordinated efforts to ensure targeted and effective delivery.
The BKJ initiative was introduced in 2023. The programme has received more than RM26 million in state government funding, with strong grassroots response driving further expansion.
This year, the initiative will benefit 750,200 families statewide through expanded coverage and additional subsidies for essential goods.
The RM15 Jualan Kombo Kasih bundle, comprising chicken, eggs and cooking oil, remains a central feature of the programme.
"This is our commitment to ensure development translates into real savings for the people, especially during challenging economic times," Onn Hafiz said.
"I am fully aware that what we are doing may still not be enough. If we are able, we want to help everyone. InsyaAllah, if state revenue continues to grow, we will increase assistance for the people."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: DAP tells election machinery to prepare for snap polls in three states
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: DAP has directed its party machinery to prepare for the possibility of snap elections in Negri Sembilan, Melaka, and Johor.
Its secretary-general Anthony Loke said the decision followed deliberations on the potential dissolution of the respective state legislative assemblies in the near future.
The decision was reached during the DAP central executive committee monthly meeting held at the party's headquarters here last night.
"The meeting discussed the latest political developments in Negri Sembilan and decided on five matters, including directing all party machinery to prepare for possible snap elections in these three states," Loke said in a statement today.
Loke said DAP remains firm in its stance not to support any proposal to replace the current menteri besar, following a failed attempt to topple the existing state administration.
"DAP will continue to provide full support to Datuk Seri Aminuddin Harun as the Negri Sembilan menteri besar.
"We always respect and uphold the institution of the constitutional monarchy. All issues involving the royal institution and Adat Pepatih must be resolved with full respect and decorum, in accordance with the Federal Constitution and the state constitution," he said.
Loke also expressed the party's gratitude to the Yang di-Pertuan Besar of Negri Sembilan, Tuanku Muhriz Tuanku Munawir, following the ruler's decree that the state government should continue to function as usual under Aminuddin's administration.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Chevron CEO says shortages in oil supply will begin appearing
Article:
HOUSTON: Chevron chairman and chief executive officer Mike Wirth said on Monday that physical shortages in oil supply would begin appearing around the world because of the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, through which 20 per cent of global crude supply passes.
Economies will begin shrinking, first in Asia, as demand adjusts to reduced supply with the strait still closed because of the US-Israeli war with Iran, Wirth said during a discussion sponsored by the Milken Institute.
"We will start to see physical shortages," Wirth said, noting that surplus supply in commercial markets, tankers in so-called shadow fleets avoiding sanctions, and national strategic reserves were being absorbed.
"Demand needs to move to meet supply," he said. "Economies are going to have to slow."
Asia is most heavily dependent on the Gulf's oil production and refineries, with Europe likely to be affected next, Wirth said.
Wirth noted that the United States, a net exporter of crude, would be less affected than other parts of the globe, but eventually the effects would be felt there as well.
He pointed out that the last scheduled shipment of oil from the Gulf was being offloaded at the Port of Long Beach, which supplies Los Angeles and southern California.
The overall effect of the Hormuz closure is "potentially as big as in the 1970s," Wirth said. Two major supply disruptions in that decade shook economies around the world, leading to fuel rationing and long lines at retail pumps.
Because of the Hormuz closure, Spirit Airlines went out of business over the weekend as the cost of jet fuel surged amid tighter supplies.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: IMF chief Georgieva warns of 'much worse outcome' if Middle East war drags into 2027
Article:
WASHINGTON: The head of the International Monetary Fund on Monday warned that inflation was already picking up and the global economy could face a "much worse outcome" if the war in the Middle East drags into 2027 and oil prices hit around US$125 per barrel.
IMF managing director Kristalina Georgieva said the continuation of the war meant that the global lender's scenario calling for a minor slowdown of global growth and a minor increase in prices was no longer possible.
As a result, the IMF's "adverse scenario" was already in effect, she said.
Long-term inflation expectations remained anchored and financial conditions were not tightening, but that could change if the war continued, she told a conference hosted by the Milken Institute.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Asia battles rising, uneven toll of energy crisis caused by Iran war
Article:
SINGAPORE: Governments in Asia, the top oil importing region, are scrambling to find alternatives and insulate their economies from the worst of the energy crisis triggered by the Iran war, but the pain is getting increasingly costly.
The disruption spurred the Asian Development Bank to cut its growth forecast for developing Asia and the Pacific to 4.7 per cent this year and 4.8 per cent in 2027, down from 5.1 per cent for both years previously, and lifted its inflation outlook to 5.2 per cent for this year.
Overall oil imports to Asia, which takes 85 per cent of Gulf crude shipments, plunged 30 per cent in April on the year, to their lowest since October 2015, Kpler data shows, after two months of the near-closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a key chokepoint for a fifth of global oil and gas supplies.
Fiscal strains are mounting across the region, particularly South Asia, as governments spend billions of US$ on subsidies and import duty waivers to compensate.
"The first line of defence ... is that the governments decided to absorb the initial shock by either providing subsidies or cutting excise duties on fuel products," said Hanna Luchnikava-Schorsch of S&P Global Market Intelligence.
India's state-dominated refining sector has kept fuel prices steady despite surging crude costs, losing about 100 rupees (US$1.06) a litre on diesel and 20 rupees on gasoline, but some analysts forecast price hikes after state polls ended in April.
Many regional governments have moved to limit fuel use or clamp down on hoarding, while several have curbed exports and many, including Australia, have espoused diplomatic efforts to ensure access.
China, the world's biggest oil importer, has shielded itself with sizeable reserves, a diverse energy supply chain and export curbs on fuel and fertiliser, although Beijing is making exceptions for some regional buyers, from Australia to Myanmar.
Even as governments tap fiscal resources, forex reserves and oil inventories, the war's economic impact on Asia has not been as bad as feared, Goldman Sachs said.
Nevertheless, it trimmed 2026 growth forecasts for Japan and some Southeast Asian countries and slightly lifted inflation expectations, while warning of a key unresolved question.
"How much of the resilience thus far reflects structural factors versus unsustainable declines in buffer stocks?" its analysts said in a note.
FIRST LINES OF DEFENCE
Asia's emerging market currencies have fallen furthest and to lower lows against the dollar, compared with global peers and the region's bigger currencies, with the peso, rupee and rupiah all making record lows.
Since the war started at the end of February, the Philippine peso has dropped more than 5 per cent, the Thai baht and rupee more than 3 per cent each and the rupiah more than 2.5 per cent.
By contrast China's yuan is the region's top performer, up 0.8 per cent against the dollar, while Japan has intervened to push up the yen, to stand 0.4 per cent higher than pre-war levels. South Korea's won is down about 1.1 per cent.
The South Asian economies of Pakistan, Bangladesh and Sri Lanka are the most vulnerable to the burdens triggered by the crunch, S&P Global Market Intelligence said.
Pakistan, for example, recently issued its first tenders since 2023 to buy liquefied natural gas.
It is looking to replace supply it is unable to source from Qatar, paying US$18.88 per million British thermal unit for one cargo, or roughly US$30 million more than market prices before the war, according to Reuters calculations.
"These countries use more of their resources on subsidising domestic public energy enterprises and basically shielding the final consumers from the energy price shock," added Luchnikava-Schorsch, the S&P unit's head of Asia-Pacific Economics.
"These are also the countries which have the slimmest fiscal buffers."
Still, regional economies are better positioned than when the start of the Ukraine war in 2022 triggered the last energy shock, she said.
COPING MECHANISMS
Responses across Asia are shaped by the circumstances of individual nations.
For example, energy producer Indonesia has told operators to prioritise the domestic market over exports and is halting LNG shipments that were not under contract.
Southeast Asia's biggest economy is also looking to Africa and Latin America to replace Middle Eastern oil, and plans to buy 150 million barrels from Russia by year-end.
In Thailand, a source at a state-owned refiner said the firm had paused crude purchases as national stocks of refined products rose after refineries stepped up output and a government ban closed off exports.
At the same time, curbs on energy use and high prices have led to falling demand, he added.
Japan, which buys 95 per cent of its oil from the Middle East, has stepped up purchases of US oil, paying spot market prices that soared after the start of the war, plus the cost of shipping from the US, which takes twice as long as from the Gulf.
On Friday, Japan began releasing 36 million barrels of crude from stockpiles, its second release since the start of the war.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US sinks Iranian boats, shoots down missiles in Strait of Hormuz operation
Article:
WASHINGTON: The US military said on Monday it destroyed six Iranian small boats and intercepted Iranian cruise missiles and drones as Tehran sought to thwart a new US naval effort to open shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.
US President Donald Trump launched the operation, called Project Freedom, on Monday as he sought to wrest control of the critical waterway from Iran, which effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz after the US and Israel started the conflict on Feb 28.
US Admiral Brad Cooper, the head of Central Command, declined to comment on whether he thought a ceasefire begun on April 8 remained in effect as Iran lashed out in region, including with drone and missile attacks on the UAE on Monday.
But Cooper acknowledged Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps efforts to "interfere" with Trump's operation.
"The IRGC has launched multiple cruise missiles, drones and small boats at ships we are protecting. We have defeated each and every one of those threats through the clinical application of defensive munitions," he said.
Cooper said he "strongly advised" Iranian forces to remain well clear of US military assets as Washington launches the operation, which he said involved 15,000 US troops, US Navy destroyers, over 100 land- and sea-based aircraft and undersea assets.
"The US commanders who are on the scene have all the authorities necessary to defend their units and to defend commercial shipping," he said.
A South Korean ship was hit by an explosion in the Strait of Hormuz on Monday, but Trump remarked in a social media post that the South Korean ship was not part of the operation and perhaps it should join US efforts to protect ship movements near Iran. Trump estimated the US had sunk seven Iranian fast boats.
The US operation to unblock the Strait of Hormuz involved multiple steps, including first clearing a pathway of Iranian mines. The US then proved the safety of the route earlier on Monday by sending two US flagged commercial ships through the strait.
Iran's Revolutionary Guards said no commercial vessels had crossed the strait in the past few hours, and that US claims to the contrary were false. Iranian state media also denied reports that the US had sunk Iranian vessels.
Cooper said the US operation went beyond a traditional escorting mission. Instead, he said it was a larger, multi-layered defensive arrangement that included ships, helicopters, aircraft and even electronic warfare to defend against Iranian threats.
He said the Iranian fast boats were sunk by US Apache and Seahawk helicopters.
"If you're escorting a ship, you're playing kind of one on one. I think we have a much better defensive arrangement in this process," he said. "We have a much broader defensive package than you would have ever if you were just escorting."
The operation is Trump's latest effort to force an end to the disruption of international energy supplies caused by Iran's blockade of the strait, which carried a fifth of global oil and liquefied natural gas before the war.
Cooper said the US military was encouraging vessels to travel through the passageway despite Iranian threats to use military force that have effectively left craft from 87 countries stranded in the Gulf.
"Over the last 12 hours, we've reached out to dozens of ships and shipping companies to encourage traffic flow through the strait," Cooper said.
"This news has been quite enthusiastically received, and we're already beginning to see movement."
The Iranian threat has left many ships stranded in the Gulf. Some 805 commercial vessels, including fuel and chemical tankers, container ships, auto carriers and bulk ships, have sent an automatic identification system (AIS) signal from within the Gulf during the last 24 hours, according to MarineTraffic data.
Cooper said a US blockade of Iran, which prevents ships from going to Iran or departing Iranian territory, also remained in effect and was exceeding expectations. - REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysian haj pilgrims diverted to Oman to leave for Jeddah on alternative flight
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The 278 haj pilgrims diverted to Oman after their Malaysia Airlines flight experienced technical issues are scheduled to depart for Jeddah on an alternative aircraft on May 6.
Tabung Haji (TH), in a statement today, said the aircraft is expected to depart for Jeddah from Muscat International Airport in Oman about 3am (Oman time).
It added that the aircraft is expected to land at King Abdulaziz International Airport in Jeddah about 5.10am (Saudi Arabian time) on the same day.
"All 278 haj pilgrims involved were provided with meals and refreshments before being transported to a hotel for rest while awaiting their new departure time.
"Personnel from Amal by Malaysia Airlines have been deployed to the airport to monitor the situation and ensure the welfare of the pilgrims," TH said.
Amal by Malaysia Airlines is the haj and umrah pilgrimage arm of Malaysia Aviation Group.
It was reported yesterday that
flight KT051 (MH8110)
was diverted to Muscat as a precautionary measure after it experienced a technical issue.
The flight had departed from Kuala Lumpur International Airport at 2.06pm on Monday and was originally scheduled to arrive in Jeddah about 5.50pm the same day.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Grab beats revenue estimates on strong delivery, mobility demand
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Singapore's Grab beat Wall Street expectations for first-quarter revenue on Tuesday, benefiting from resilient demand for its ride-hailing and food-delivery services after rolling out promotional offers and bundled features to attract more customers.
Southeast Asia's biggest ride-hailing and delivery firm has been trying to boost spending on its platform by incorporating artificial intelligence features into its superapp and bundling its core ride-hailing, delivery and financial services.
With oil and gas prices soaring due to the war in the Middle East, the company has also promoted more affordable offerings, including a "saver" option, to woo customers grappling with higher costs of living.
About 35 per cent of its users are on the "saver" programme, opting for cheaper deliveries, CFO Peter Oey told Reuters.
"It is a very good balance between the price-sensitive customers and for those who are less price-sensitive. That gives us the levers also to continue to make sure that our margin for our delivery business continues to improve," he said.
Grab's quarterly revenue rose 24 per cent from a year earlier to US$955 million, compared with analysts' estimates of US$921.1 million, according to data compiled by LSEG.
Deliveries revenue increased 23 per cent to US$510 million, while mobility revenue was up 19 per cent at US$337 million.
Shares of the company were up 1.6 per cent in extended trading.
On-demand gross merchandise value rose 24 per cent to US$6.1 billion, as growth in both mobility and deliveries accelerated despite seasonal softness typically associated with regional festive periods.
Profit came in at US$120 million, compared with US$10 million a year earlier.
The company faces heavy competition in the Southeast Asian market, with rivals also turning to AI and promotional offers to attract potential customers.
Earlier this year, Grab agreed to buy Delivery Hero's Foodpanda delivery business in Taiwan for US$600 million, a major expansion that allows it to capture market share outside its home region.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Canada separatists submit bid to force Alberta independence vote
Article:
EDMONTON: Alberta separatists submitted stacks of boxes to election officials on Monday, which they said contained the signatures of more than 300,000 supporters, enough names to force an independence referendum in Canada's oil-rich province.
A boisterous crowd of several hundred waved the blue provincial flag on a bright, clear day, cheering as separatist leader Mitch Sylvestre delivered the petitions to Elections Alberta.
"We're not like the rest of Canada," Sylvestre told AFP.
"We're 100 percent conservative. We're being ruled by Liberals who don't think like us."
"They're trying to shut down our industry," he said, referring to the lucrative provincial oil sector.
The pro-independence camp in the western province of five million people had existed on the margins for decades, but the movement has gathered pace in recent months and is closer than ever to triggering a vote.
Polls put separatist support at roughly 30 per cent, but even if the federalist side wins a prospective referendum, leaders on both sides say the process has left Canada permanently changed.
- 'TREASON' -
Standing outside the provincial legislature on a grey Sunday evening, former Alberta deputy premier and activist for the federalist side, Thomas Lukaszuk, told AFP he now struggles to look at the blue provincial flag that fluttered behind him, saying it has been co-opted by separatists.
"It stands for something that most of us Albertans and Canadians don't stand for. It's a form of treason," said Lukaszuk, who moved to Canada as a child when his family sought refuge from communist Poland.
Lukaszuk, who is spearheading Alberta's Forever Canadian campaign, said the ongoing separatist movement has been helped by a right-wing premier, Danielle Smith, who has sought closer ties with President Donald Trump's Republican Party.
Tacit support from Washington, including several State Department meetings, has also helped, he said.
"The separatists are not elected members. They're just citizens of Canada residing in Alberta, and they actually formed delegations and are received by the highest levels of US administration. That must be very empowering to them," he told AFP.
US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent also drew Canadian furore in January when he said Alberta and the United States would make "natural partners."
- 'FABRIC' DIVIDED -
Sylvestre heads Stay Free Alberta, which had until the beginning of May to collect 178,000 signatures.
While he says the group easily cleared that bar, Elections Alberta needs to verify the names – a process placed on hold by a court ruling.
Indigenous groups in Alberta who signed treaties with Britain before Canada secured independence – have filed a legal challenge, arguing that separating Alberta would violate their treaty rights, a case that could render the entire process moot.
But Michael Wagner, an independent historian and long-standing supporter of Albertan independence, told AFP last week that even if his side loses, the movement "is not going to just disappear."
"I think this is going to be a permanent change in our political culture."
- OIL POLITICS -
Alberta joined the Canadian confederation in 1905 and resentments towards eastern political leaders in Ontario and Quebec fuelled marginal separatist movements at various points over the last century.
But anger at Ottawa intensified in protest against former prime minister Pierre Trudeau's 1980 National Energy Program, which broadened Ottawa's control over the oil industry.
The programme included new taxes giving Ottawa more revenue from Alberta's oil as a safeguard following the oil price shocks of the 1970s.
Wagner called the program a "game-changer" which entrenched the idea of independence.
Fast-forward 35 years, and Trudeau's son Justin was elected prime minister with a climate-conscious agenda reviled by many in Alberta.
Standing outside Elections Alberta on Monday, separatist supporter Tammy Kaleta told AFP "the Trudeau regime" – both Pierre and Justin – had driven her towards independence.
"This movement really inspired me," the 64-year-old said.
Some independence supporters want to join the United States – Alberta borders the US state of Montana – but the group's leaders say they are merely seeking support from Trump to help secure an exit from Canada.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RM138m expansion for Bantuan Kasih Johor
Article:
ISKANDAR PUTERI:  The Johor government has reinforced its commitment to rakyat welfare with the expansion of Bantuan Kasih Johor (BKJ) Phase 4, a RM138 million initiative expected to benefit more than 600,000 Bangsa Johor statewide.
BKJ is a financial initiative introduced in 2023, shortly after Datuk Onn Hafiz Ghazi assumed office as  menteri besar on March 15, 2022.
The initiative is a state-led umbrella assistance programme designed to deliver direct aid to the rakyat through structured phases.
Since then, it has been rolled out in successive stages, namely Phases 1, 2 and 3, and now Phase 4, with expanded reach and value in line with Johor's strengthening fiscal position.
Onn Hafiz said the latest phase was formulated based on comprehensive feedback gathered from previous implementations, ensuring that assistance is more targeted, inclusive and impactful, reaching a wider segment of society.
"The state government has received extensive feedback from BKJ Phases 1, 2 and 3.
"Every time we go to the ground and engage with the people, we receive suggestions to ensure more Bangsa Johor can benefit directly," he said during the state legislative assembly sitting  at the Sultan Ismail Building in Kota Iskandar here  yesterday.
[[nid:1432426]]
He said the improved BKJ Phase 4 reflected the state's continued efforts to ensure that growth and development were translated into direct relief for the people.
A total of 22 initiatives have been introduced under the latest phase, covering a broad spectrum of society, including senior citizens, homemakers, young couples, small traders, persons with disabilities, orphans, community leaders and volunteers.
Onn Hafiz (BN-Machap) said this in response to questions on the cost of living and rakyat's welfare  by several state assemblymen, including Nor Rashidah Ramli (BN-Parit Raja), Tan Chong (BN-Bekok) and Anuar Abd Manap (BN-Pemanis).
He said among the key components are RM200 cash assistance for over 307,000 senior citizens, RM200 for 136,000 non-working homemakers and RM400 for 45,000 newly married couples.
Small traders and licensed petty businesses will also receive RM300 each, benefiting nearly 30,000 recipients to support their economic sustainability.
The programme further extends support to other targeted groups, including students with disabilities, orphans, low-income household heads registered under eKasih, school transport drivers, welfare recipients, mosque and surau committees and unremunerated religious officers.
eKasih is the national poverty database system used to identify and register low-income households so they can receive targeted government assistance.
BKJ also ensures frontliners and essential service groups such as hospital security personnel, cleaning staff, religious facility workers, homestay operators, volunteer grave diggers, youth associations and state sports bodies are included.
Onn Hafiz said the initiative reflected the state's broader objective of strengthening social protection systems so that no segment of society is left behind.
"The state government is committed to ensuring that every increase in revenue is returned to the people through high-impact initiatives that are truly felt by Bangsa Johor."
He added that the government remained open to further enhancements in line with fiscal capacity.
"I am fully aware that what we are doing today may still not be enough. If we are able, we want to help everyone. InsyaAllah, if state revenue continues to grow, we will expand assistance further for the people."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Musk agrees to pay US$1.5mil over Twitter share buying
Article:
WASHINGTON: Elon Musk on Monday agreed to pay a small amount to end a government lawsuit that accused him of breaking stock market rules when he was secretly buying up shares of Twitter before his US$44 billion takeover of the social media platform in 2022.
Musk's trust will pay a US$1.5 million fine under the deal, which was filed in a Washington federal court and still needs a judge's approval.
The case involves Musk's missing a statutory deadline for notifying regulators as he bought more and more Twitter shares ahead of the takeover.
Musk's attorney Alex Spiro cast the outcome as a vindication, saying his client "has now been cleared of all issues related to the late filing of forms in the Twitter acquisition, as we said from the outset he would be."
"A trust vehicle has agreed to a small fine for being late on one filing," Spiro said.
In an email to AFP, Spiro added that the deal with the US government was not a settlement "because he did nothing wrong."
"The case against him was dismissed," Spiro said.
The case centred on a simple rule: when an investor buys more than five percent of a publicly traded company, they are required by law to disclose that stake within 10 days.
The Securities and Exchange Commission, the federal agency that polices financial markets, in January 2025 said Musk blew past that deadline by 11 days when he was building up his position in Twitter in early 2022.
The SEC said Musk's delay allowed him to keep buying at bargain prices, saving himself an estimated US$150 million at the expense of other shareholders who sold without knowing what was happening.
Despite those allegations, the deal with the SEC does not require Musk to pay back any of those savings.
His trust agreed only to the US$1.5 million penalty and a promise not to violate the same rule again – without admitting he did anything wrong.
The SEC said it amended its complaint to add Musk's trust as a defendant and filed the proposed settlement at the same time. If the judge signs off, the agency said it will drop Musk personally from the case, ending it entirely.
The lawsuit was first filed just days before then-president Joe Biden left office.
Musk, a close ally of President Donald Trump at the time, tried to get it thrown out, but a federal judge rejected that effort in February.
The fine comes on the heels of a separate trial in California, where a jury in March found that Musk had misled Twitter investors with misleading posts during the chaotic 2022 takeover.
Damages in that case could reach roughly US$2 billion, although Musk's lawyers have said they plan to appeal.
It is the second time Musk has settled with the SEC.
In 2018, he paid US$20 million and gave up his role as Tesla's chairman after claiming on social media that he had the funding to take the electric car maker private – a deal that never materialised.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor rolls out RM170m assistance
Article:
ISKANDAR PUTERI: A surge in state revenue has enabled the Johor s government to roll out an expanded slate of people-centric assistance exceeding RM170 million.
Menteri Besar Datuk Onn Hafiz Ghazi remained resolute in his commitment to ensure that every ringgit of the state's growing revenue is channelled back to the people to cushion the burden of rising living costs and safeguard the well-being of Bangsa Johor statewide.
State revenue hit RM1 billion up to April this year, an increase of RM307 million compared with the same period last year.
Onn Hafiz said Johor has moved immediately to channel the gains directly to the rakyat through high-impact initiatives under its Bantuan Kasih Johor (BKJ) framework.
BKJ is more than a financial initiative; it is a promise of care in action, where the state's growing prosperity is returned to the people in the form of dignity, relief and shared hope.
Introduced in 2023 as a state-led umbrella assistance programme, BKJ is designed to channel direct aid to the people through structured phases.
Since its introduction, it has been rolled out in multiple stages (Phases 1, 2 and  3, and now Phase 4), steadily expanding in scope and value as the state's financial position strengthened.
[[nid:1432411]]
Onn Hafiz said BKJ was born out of a simple principle: as Johor grows, its people must grow with it and not be left behind.
Central to the latest push is BKJ Phase Four, backed by RM138 million and set to benefit more than 600,000 people  across a wide spectrum of society, from senior citizens and homemakers to small traders, students, and vulnerable groups.
The expanded assistance comes amid continued economic headwinds globally, with the state government acknowledging cost-of-living pressures while maintaining that Johor remains on a strong fiscal footing, underpinned by record investments, robust job creation and steady growth.
In tandem, Johor has significantly scaled up its Jualan Kasih Johor programme, increasing the allocation to RM45.4 million.
The move is expected to benefit over 750,000 families through subsidised essential goods.
Introduced in 2023, the programme provides essential goods at subsidised prices to help ease the cost of living for households across the state.
The initiative, which includes the popular RM15 household combo comprising chicken, eggs and cooking oil, has gained traction at the grassroots level, prompting the additional RM30 million injection to widen its reach statewide.
Beyond immediate relief, Johor has committed RM137.8 million this year under its Perumahan Kasih Johor (PKJ) initiative, aimed at improving access to affordable housing through cash aid for first-time buyers, renters and those in need of home repairs.
The PKJ initiative provides targeted financial assistance to make housing more affordable and accessible for eligible families.
The state's broader fiscal strength has been driven by a string of milestones, including record-breaking investments, more than 80,000 job opportunities created, and a 6.4 per cent gross domestic product  growth rate, positioning Johor as the highest in the country.
Against this backdrop, Onn Hafiz reiterated his pledge to ensure that every increment in state revenue translates into tangible benefits for the people, with further assistance expected should the upward trajectory continue.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia to kick off B15 rollout with 19 plants in June
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The government has decided that B15 biodiesel will be produced by 19 licensed plants from June 1, in an effort to lower diesel prices nationwide.
An expert said Malaysia's supply fundamentals for the initiative are encouraging and production capacity is not a constraint.
"(The 19) plants are capable of producing around 23 million-24 million tonnes. But because the country has been operating under a B10 standard, that capacity has been significantly underutilised," ESG Malaysia executive director Dr Harald Sippel told Business Times.
The government estimates that moving to B15 would require about 800,000 tonnes of crude palm oil, an additional 400,000 tonnes above the current B10 requirement.
However, he said the main challenge lies in blending infrastructure.
"I hear that inspections have confirmed that more than 70 per cent of depots can execute the blending using existing facilities and depot operators are currently making operational adjustments to ensure supply security.
"But what about the rest? That remaining 30 per cent is where attention is needed. Done well, this transition is very manageable," he added.
On Monday, Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Dr Ahmad Zahid Hamidi, who is also in charge of the Rural and Regional Development Ministry, said production would be introduced in stages, beginning with B15 before progressing to B20, with the potential of reaching B50 within the next two to three years.
"This phased approach is intended to ensure that crude palm oil (CPO) prices do not place pressure on biodiesel production costs," he said at the ministry's monthly assembly.
Ahmad Zahid said palm oil-based biodiesel could serve as a new catalyst for the rural economy, beyond supporting the national energy agenda.
Meanwhile, Sippel said that a B15 biodiesel represents a structural shift in how the country thinks about energy security and economic resilience.
"Diesel prices in Peninsular Malaysia have surged dramatically in recent months, and the government has identified higher biodiesel blending as a critical national economic defence strategy. This is quite significant, it is not merely an alternative energy policy."
He said that raising the blend from B10 to B15 or even B20 could save Malaysia about RM1.5 billion annually in fuel subsidies.
For businesses, particularly logistics and transport operators, a more stable domestic fuel price anchored partly to locally-produced palm oil is far preferable to one hostage to global oil shocks.
"Malaysia is also considering moving to B50 within a few years. This is where the ESG narrative becomes very relevant - not just for domestic audiences, but for international ones.
"As Malaysia builds a more mature, higher-blend domestic biodiesel ecosystem with documented sustainability credentials, it strengthens its hand in those conversations.
"A country that consumes its own palm oil responsibly at scale is a far more credible export partner than one that simply ships the commodity out," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Petronas Gas to build Malaysia's third regasification terminal
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Petronas Gas Bhd (PetGas) is set to develop Malaysia's third regasification terminal in Lumut, Perak, marking a major expansion of the country's gas infrastructure at an estimated multi-billion ringgit cost.
PetGas today said it had received a notification letter from the Economy Ministry dated April 30 to undertake the project under the floating storage and regasification unit (FSRU) concept.
The company is evaluating the project and may undertake with a strategic partner, although details have not been finalised.
The proposed terminal would follow two large-scale plants that collectively might have cost up to RM9 billion and form the backbone of the country's gas import system.
The first, the Sungai Udang Regasification Terminal (RGT-1) in Melaka commissioned in 2013, was built at an estimated RM3 billion-RM4 billion.
It has a capacity of about 3.8 million tonnes per annum, supplying roughly 500-550 million standard cubic feet per day (mmscfd) to Peninsular Malaysia.
The second, the Pengerang LNG Regasification Terminal (RGT-2) in Johor, which began operations in 2017, is larger and cost about RM4 billion-RM5 billion.
It has a capacity of 3.5 million-5.0 million tonnes per annum or up to 700 mmscfd.
Both facilities are operated by Petroliam Nasional Bhd (Petronas) and are critical in ensuring energy security as domestic gas production declines while industrial and power demand continues to rise.
The proposed Lumut facility is expected to further strengthen Peninsular Malaysia's gas supply resilience, particularly for power generation and industrial users, amid rising reliance on imported LNG.
PetGas said the project is aligned with its long-term strategy to strengthen Malaysia's gas infrastructure and ensure sufficient supply to meet rising demand in the peninsula.
RGT-3 will adopt the FSRU concept where liquefied natural gas (LNG) is stored and converted back into gas offshore before being delivered to the national pipeline network.
The regasified LNG will be transported via a connecting pipeline from the terminal's offshore and onshore facilities to the Peninsular Gas Utilisation (PGU) system.
PetGas said the FSRU-based design is expected to enhance flexibility and speed of deployment compared with conventional onshore terminals, while also improving Malaysia's ability to respond to fluctuating gas demand.
The company had previously conducted technical and commercial assessments on new gas infrastructure requirements based on Peninsular Malaysia's projected supply-demand outlook.
Following these studies, it submitted a proposal for RGT-3 to the Energy Commission, targeting a commercial operation date in the second quarter of 2029.
Once completed, RGT-3 is expected to complement Malaysia's existing regasification system and strengthen energy security in the peninsula, particularly as domestic gas production gradually declines while demand from power generation and industry continues to grow.
PetGas is the operator of gas transportation and regasification infrastructure and the addition of RGT-3 will further expand its role in supporting the country's transition toward a more flexible and import-reliant gas supply system.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: US–Iran ceasefire teeters as UAE reports missile and drone attacks
Article:
TEHERAN: A ceasefire between Iran and the United States was teetering Monday as the two countries traded fire over the strategic Strait of Hormuz and the United Arab Emirates reported attacks for the first time since the truce was declared nearly a month ago.
A day after President Donald Trump announced an operation to escort trapped vessels through the strait, Fox News quoted him as threatening that Iran would be "blown off the face of the earth" if it attacks US ships.
Iran appeared undaunted as it vowed to keep exerting control over the Strait of Hormuz, the narrow waterway through which one-fifth of the world's oil flowed before the United States and Israel attacked Iran on Feb 28.
A US admiral said US forces sank six small Iranian ships. Iran denied any had been sunk and earlier fired warning shots at US warships.
The UAE, a close US ally and key Arab partner of Israel, said it came under a barrage of missiles and drones from Iran.
"These attacks represent a dangerous escalation and an unacceptable transgression, posing a direct threat to the state's security, stability, and the safety of its territories," the UAE's foreign ministry said in a statement.
A strike targeting an energy installation in the emirate of Fujairah injured three Indian nationals, UAE authorities said.
Two people were also injured when a residential building was hit in Oman's Bukha along the coastline of the Strait of Hormuz, state media reported.
- RETURN TO WAR FOOTING -
Oil prices climbed further after the UAE attack, with the benchmark international contract Brent crude for July delivery jumping more than five percent.
The soaring energy costs for consumers since the war have caused economic pain around the world and created a political headache for Trump months before congressional elections.
In Israel, a military official said forces were on high alert.
The UAE ordered all schools to return to remote learning for the rest of the week.
According to the UAE defence ministry, four cruise missiles were launched from Iran, with three successfully shot down and another falling into the water.
Iran also fired drones at a tanker affiliated with its state-owned oil giant ADNOC, UAE authorities said.
A senior Iranian military official did not deny the strikes but said that the Islamic republic had "no pre-planned programme to attack the oil facilities in question."
"What happened was the product of the US military's adventurism to create a passage for ships to illegally pass through" the Strait of Hormuz, the official said, according to state television.
"The US military must be held accountable for it," the official added.
But Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi – seen as a moderate in the cleric-run state in which top leaders have been killed by Israel – said that the clashes in the strait showed there was "no military solution to a political crisis" and pointed to Pakistan's efforts to keep mediating.
"The US should be wary of being dragged back into quagmire by ill-wishers. So should the UAE. Project Freedom is Project Deadlock," he wrote on X.
- US FLEXES MUSCLE IN STRAIT -
Trump has repeatedly demanded that Iran reopen the Strait of Hormuz – which was open before the war and which Teheran considers a main point of leverage.
On Sunday, Trump announced what he called "Project Freedom" to guide ships from neutral countries out of the Gulf, saying it was a humanitarian effort to help their stranded crews.
Much remained unclear about how the plan would operate and how the United States would assist.
US Central Command said Monday that guided-missile destroyers had transited Hormuz and that, as a first step in "Project Freedom", two US-flagged merchant vessels had travelled out of the Gulf.
But Iran's Revolutionary Guards denied the US statement, saying: "No commercial vessels or oil tankers have passed through the Strait of Hormuz in the past few hours."
Seoul said on Monday that an "explosion and fire" had struck a South Korean ship in the strait.
- TRUMP PLAYS DOWN IMPACT -
Trump had appeared to play down the Iranian strikes, writing on social media that Iran had "taken some shots" but that it caused little damage.
As of April 29, more than 900 commercial vessels were located in the Gulf, according to maritime intelligence firm AXSMarine.
Diplomacy between Washington and Teheran has been deadlocked since the ceasefire, with the United States twice aborting plans for senior officials to attend new talks in Pakistan and Trump voicing doubt about an Iranian proposal.
Another ceasefire has also come under strain, in Lebanon, which Israel has heavily bombed and invaded with ground troops in response to fire into Israel by Iranian-backed Hizbollah.
The Israeli strikes have killed more than 2,700 people, according to Lebanon's health ministry.
Hizbollah and Israeli troops clashed in southern Lebanon on Monday, with Israeli troops reporting moderate injuries to two of its soldiers.
Lebanese President Joseph Aoun called for a security deal and an end to Israeli attacks before any meeting with Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, a potentially historic encounter which Trump has proposed to take place this month at the White House.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Armed man shot by US Secret Service near White House
Article:
WASHINGTON: An armed man was shot by US Secret Service (USSS) agents in downtown Washington on Monday, officials said, prompting a brief lockdown of the nearby White House.
The shooting near the National Mall occurred shortly after a motorcade carrying Vice President JD Vance had passed by the area, USSS Deputy Director Matthew Quinn said.
Quinn told reporters he did not believe that the vice president was an intended target and said he could not speculate if it was related to recent attempts on the life of President Donald Trump.
"I'm not going to guess on that," he said. "Whether or not it was directed to the president or not, I don't know, but we will find out."
Quinn said the shooting took place after Secret Service agents identified a "suspicious individual," who appeared to have a firearm.
The man fled on foot after being approached by the officers, drew his weapon and opened fire, Quinn said.
The Secret Service agents returned fire and wounded the suspect. He was taken to the hospital. His condition was not immediately known.
Quinn said a bystander, a juvenile, was slightly wounded.
The incident comes slightly more than a week after a gunman tried to break through security at a Washington hotel where Trump was attending an event.
Cole Allen, 31, has been charged with seeking to assassinate the president.--AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Couple killed in suspected drink-driving crash in Jalan Ampang dreamt of owning a home [WATCH]
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A woman killed alongside her husband by an alleged drink-driver  on Saturday morning had  spent her entire life working towards  her dream of owning a house.
A. Menagah, 56, managed to   place a downpayment for the property,  with her son as a co-owner, six months ago.
However, she would never get to live in the house.
Menagah and her husband A. Doraisingam, 61,  were riding a motorcycle when  the accident happened at 5.30am in Jalan Ampang.
Doraisingam was  sending Menagah to work at a private hospital nearby.  Menagah   worked in the food and beverage department,  and was supposed to celebrate her 30th work anniversary  in August.
The couple's  motorcycle was hit by a  car in a suspected drink-driving case. Both victims  were flung from their motorcycle to the road pavement.
The driver and one of the two occupants of the car are  receiving treatment at the Kuala Lumpur Hospital, while  another passenger received outpatient treatment.
Kuala Lumpur Traffic Enforcement and Investigation Department chief Assistant Commissioner Mohd Zamzuri Mohd Isa has said initial investigations found that the driver of the car was suspected to be under the influence of alcohol.
He said the driver's blood and urine samples had been sent to the Chemistry Department.
The three children of Doraisingam and Menagah told the
New Straits Times
this was their parents' routine  on a working day.
"My father was a retired security guard and he always sent my mother to work on his motorcycle from our rented flat in Jalan Raja Laut," said the  daughter,  D. Pavithra.
The 24-year-old, who graduated with a diploma in early childhood education in January, said  on the day of the incident, Menagah came into her room and reminded  a technician  would come  to repair their television.
"That was the last time I spoke to her," Pavithra said, adding that she is grateful her parents were able to witness her graduation earlier this year.
She described  her parents as affectionate and devoted, even if they occasionally argued.
"They would have small fights, usually because of us," she said, adding that she was  close to her father. "I was my father's pet."
Pavithra's elder brother, D. Shathiswaran, 26, a technician, said the family was still reeling from the shock and demanded accountability.
"There are closed-circuit television cameras along the road and a dashcam in the suspect's vehicle. Hopefully, the recordings will shed some light on what  transpired."
The family was alerted  by an eyewitness who found Menagah's handphone and called several numbers listed on it.
"I was asleep at home when my uncle called about the incident," he said, adding that he and two of his uncles then rushed to the scene.
The couple has a third child, Cheran, 19, who recently enrolled in a business foundation programme at Universiti Tun Abdul Razak.
"It is  unacceptable for the children to lose both parents in the blink of an eye," said a  relative.
Menagah's younger brother, Nagulan, said his sister had long dreamt of owning her own home and  the couple were in the midst of carrying out some basic renovations before moving in.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd
